overview.summary: Brazil is home to the largest African diaspora population outside of Africa, with over 100 million people of African descent. The country's rich cultural tapestry is deeply woven with African traditions, from music and dance to cuisine and religious practices.
overview.historicalContext: Between the 16th and 19th centuries, Brazil received approximately 4.9 million enslaved Africans - more than any other country in the Americas. This massive forced migration brought diverse African cultures, languages, and traditions that have profoundly shaped Brazilian society.

keyFacts: Last country in the Americas to abolish slavery (1888)
Received 40% of all enslaved Africans brought to the Americas
Home to the largest Carnival celebration in the world
Portuguese colonial language mixed with African linguistic influences

music.name: Samba
music.description: Brazil's most iconic music and dance form, born in the favelas of Rio de Janeiro from African rhythms and Portuguese melodies.
music.africanRoots: Derived from Angolan semba dance and West African polyrhythmic traditions
music.significance: Became a symbol of Brazilian national identity and cultural resistance

music.name: Capoeira
music.description: A martial art that combines elements of dance, acrobatics, and music, developed by enslaved Africans in Brazil.
music.africanRoots: Combines fighting techniques from Angola with spiritual and musical elements
music.significance: Represents resistance, freedom, and the preservation of African warrior traditions

music.name: Axé Music
music.description: Popular music genre from Bahia that blends African rhythms with Caribbean and Brazilian influences.
music.africanRoots: Based on Yoruba religious music and West African percussion patterns
music.significance: Celebrates Afro-Brazilian identity and spiritual connection to African ancestors

food.name: Feijoada
food.description: Brazil's national dish, a hearty black bean stew with pork and beef, reflecting African cooking techniques.
food.africanRoots: Based on African one-pot cooking methods and use of beans as protein source
food.significance: Represents the fusion of African, Portuguese, and indigenous culinary traditions

food.name: Acarajé
food.description: Deep-fried balls of black-eyed pea dough filled with shrimp, popular in Bahia.
food.africanRoots: Direct descendant of West African akara, brought by Yoruba people
food.significance: Sacred food in Afro-Brazilian religions and symbol of cultural continuity

food.name: Moqueca
food.description: Traditional Brazilian seafood stew cooked in coconut milk and palm oil.
food.africanRoots: Cooking technique and use of dendê (palm oil) brought from West Africa
food.significance: Demonstrates African influence on Brazilian coastal cuisine

religion.name: Candomblé
religion.description: Afro-Brazilian religious tradition that preserves West and Central African spiritual practices.
religion.africanRoots: Combines Yoruba, Fon, and Bantu religious traditions from West and Central Africa
religion.significance: Maintained African spiritual connections and provided community strength during slavery

religion.name: Umbanda
religion.description: Syncretic religion blending African, Indigenous, and Catholic elements.
religion.africanRoots: Incorporates African spirit possession and ancestor veneration practices
religion.significance: Represents Brazilian religious syncretism and African spiritual resilience

language.name: African Linguistic Influence
language.description: Hundreds of African words have been incorporated into Brazilian Portuguese, especially in Bahia.
language.africanRoots: Primarily from Yoruba, Kikongo, and Kimbundu languages
language.significance: Demonstrates the deep linguistic impact of African cultures on Brazilian Portuguese

festivals.name: Carnival
festivals.description: Brazil's most famous festival, heavily influenced by African rhythms, dance, and celebration traditions.
festivals.africanRoots: Incorporates African drumming, call-and-response singing, and circular dance patterns
festivals.significance: Showcases African cultural resilience and creativity on a global stage

festivals.name: Festa de Iemanjá
festivals.description: Annual celebration honoring the Yoruba goddess of the sea, celebrated on February 2nd.
festivals.africanRoots: Direct continuation of Yoruba religious festivals honoring Yemoja
festivals.significance: Maintains direct connection to African spiritual traditions and oceanic reverence

featuredImage.alt: Colorful Brazilian Carnival dancers in traditional costumes
featuredImage.caption: Brazilian Carnival showcases the vibrant fusion of African and Brazilian cultures

gallery.alt: Capoeira practitioners performing in a roda circle
gallery.caption: Capoeira combines martial arts, dance, and music in African tradition

gallery.alt: Traditional Brazilian feijoada dish with black beans and meat
gallery.caption: Feijoada represents the fusion of African cooking techniques with local ingredients

gallery.alt: Candomblé ceremony with traditional white clothing and offerings
gallery.caption: Candomblé preserves West African spiritual traditions in Brazil
